sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, a 45-year-old male, presented with an ankle injury sustained while playing basketball with his 10-year-old son.
He reported landing awkwardly after being fouled by his son and twisting his ankle, resulting in pain and swelling on the outside of the ankle.
He has been experiencing trouble walking and feels instability in the ankle, which has disrupted his recent progress in getting back into working out.
The patient's son is a big fan of Steph Curry and enjoys playing basketball, and the patient is happy to see him involved in a sport he enjoys.
The patient mentioned that he is also coaching his son's basketball team starting in April.
Past medical history: [NOT MENTIONED]
Review of systems: [NOT MENTIONED]
Current medications: [NOT MENTIONED]
Objective:
Vital signs: [NOT MENTIONED]
Physical exam: The patient's ankle was found to have exquisite tenderness on the lateral side, with tenderness also present on the medial deltoid region.
Swelling was noted on the lateral side of the ankle, but skin integrity was intact.
Capillary refill was brisk, and there was no malalignment.
The patient reported pain and instability when standing and walking on the ankle.
Test Results: An X-ray of the right ankle showed a displaced lateral malleolus at the Weber C level, with no evidence of medial or posterior malleolar fractures.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has a lateral malleolar fracture, which is likely to require surgery to stabilize the ankle.
Problem list: Lateral malleolar fracture; active.
Plan:
I prescribed meloxicam to help manage pain and reduce swelling.
I advised the patient to ice the ankle and keep it elevated, particularly during work hours since he works from home.
I also recommended using crutches to avoid putting weight on the ankle.
I informed the patient that he will likely need to sit out for about three months, but can continue coaching his son's basketball team in April.
I encouraged the patient to continue his coaching duties and will reassess his ankle in three months to determine when he can return to exercise.
Follow-up: I will see the patient in three months to reassess his ankle and determine when he can return to exercise.
